title: Incursa.Quic Public API Surface Promotion Work Item
summary: Promote the connection-, listener-, and first client-entry Incursa.Quic consumer facade, prove the supported positive loopback establishment boundary, refine its lifecycle semantics from Microsoft test evidence, and hide the helper-layer implementation seams.
planned_changes:
- Add the public connection, stream, listener, client-option, error, and abort/direction types named in the slice.
- Promote the first server-side listener entry surface (`ListenAsync`, `AcceptConnectionAsync`) and the first honest client-entry surface (`ConnectAsync(...)`).
- Reuse the existing runtime endpoint and endpoint-host seams to build a narrow client host shell over a connected UDP socket and the supported positive loopback establishment boundary.
- Correct `QuicStreamType` to the approved direction-only public model.
- Keep the packet, frame, TLS, handshake, runtime, recovery, congestion, stream-identity, and lifecycle helper surfaces internal.
- Reconcile the public API analyzer files with the approved smaller public boundary.
- Reject unsupported `SslClientAuthenticationOptions` settings explicitly instead of silently ignoring them.
- Encode the close/dispose, error projection, read-side, listener-accept, positive loopback establishment, pending-connect cancellation, and option-shape semantics reflected by the Microsoft reference tests and the existing runtime/stream/listener-host seams.
out_of_scope: Adding the stream-entry-point and capacity-callback slice in this pass.
Adding the full client-auth contract in this pass.
Adding new transport features beyond the approved facade.
verification_plan: Use API-diff and reflection-based surface checks, plus compile-time access tests, to prove the approved public types are visible and the listed seam types are not. Add focused consumer-behavior tests for `QuicStreamType` shape, close/dispose/error projection, read-side terminal behavior, listener setup validation, listener disposal semantics, accept cancellation, supported positive loopback establishment, pending client-connect cancellation/disposal semantics, unsupported TLS rejection, and the connection/stream/listener/client facade mapping to `QuicConnectionRuntime`, `QuicConnectionStreamState`, `QuicListenerHost`, `QuicConnectionRuntimeEndpoint`, and `QuicConnectionEndpointHost`. Keep the proof focused on surface shape, positive-establishment honesty, and terminal behavior rather than full wire success; the stream-entry follow-on, fuzzing, and benchmarks remain separate slices.
completion_notes: This item now includes the first server-side public entry surface plus the supported positive client-entry boundary. The internal transport engine stays intact, and stream entry points, richer TLS policy, and the broader data path remain open.

supplemental_sections.heading: Reference Behavioral Evidence
supplemental_sections.content:
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\QuicListenerTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\QuicConnectionTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\QuicStreamTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\QuicStreamConnectedStreamConformanceTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\MsQuicCipherSuitesPolicyTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\MsQuicTests.cs`

These reference tests are the behavioral evidence for the initial public surface shape and the lifecycle semantics that are now implemented and proven in the target repo.
